Title: The β-cyanoalanine synthase pathway: beyond cyanide detoxification

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Maintains cyanide homeostasis and supports root hair formation, stress tolerance, and pathogen response through β-CAS pathway activity.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Pyridoxal phosphate-dependent β-substituted alanine synthase family
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtCysC1 encodes the mitochondrial β-cyanoalanine synthase in Arabidopsis. 2. Loss of AtCysC1 causes cyanide-associated defects in root hair formation/elongation and altered pathogen responses. 3. The gene links cyanide detoxification with nitrogen/sulfur metabolism and stress biology.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA insertion mutant; metabolite/physiological phenotyping; localization studies summarized in review
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in shoots, leaves and roots; stress-inducible under water deficit in Arabidopsis seedlings
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Water deficit; pathogen infection; endogenous ethylene-associated cyanide
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although β-CAS is treated as a pathway enzyme, Arabidopsis AtCysC1 is also discussed for broad physiological roles beyond direct catalysis in this review.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for the second step of the β-CAS pathway; impaired cyanide metabolism in nit4 mutants causes root defects and increased sensitivity to water deficit.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NIT4 mediates β-cyanoalanine conversion in the second step of the β-CAS pathway. 2. Arabidopsis nit4 mutants show impaired cyanide metabolism and root physiological defects. 3. nit4 mutants accumulate cyanide and are more sensitive to water deficit.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; GFP localization; heterologous complementation by bacterial pinA summarized in review
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Present in tissues including shoots, leaves and roots in Arabidopsis; implicated under drought and root development contexts
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Water deficit; endogenous cyanide during growth/development
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: EREBP-like proteins (nitrilase-like proteins reported to interact in tobacco model)
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review describes the enzyme as bifunctional nitrilase/nitrile hydratase or nitrilase/nitrile reductase in different summarized studies; exact mechanistic annotation may vary across reports.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; spinach; soybean; multiple higher plants
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Spinacia oleracea; Glycine max
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: β-cyanoalanine synthase pathway / cyanide assimilation pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Substitutes the sulfhydryl group of cysteine with cyanide to form β-cyanoalanine and release hydrogen sulfide.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms pathway intermediate leading to asparagine, aspartate and ammonium
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Principal first step of plant cyanide detoxification; key control step supported in review
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Detoxifies cyanide and assimilates cyanide nitrogen into amino acid metabolism; contributes to growth, development, stress tolerance and sulfur-linked signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Pyridoxal phosphate-dependent β-substituted alanine synthase family; related to O-acetylserine sulfhydrylase
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. β-CAS is the principal enzyme initiating cyanide detoxification/assimilation in plants. 2. It converts cyanide plus cysteine to β-cyanoalanine and H2S in mitochondria. 3. Its activity is widespread across tissues and species and is associated with cyanogenic metabolism, ethylene-associated cyanide, stress responses, and nitrogen/sulfur integration.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical enzyme assays; structural studies; mutant studies; localization studies summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in roots, stems, tubers, leaves, buds, cotyledons, fruits and seeds; often elevated in mesophyll and in tissues rich in cyanogenic glycosides or during flower/fruit maturation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ethylene production; drought; salinity; ozone; low temperature; pathogen infection; herbicide treatment; exogenous cyanide/cyanide complexes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Multiple plant tissues including leaves, roots, seeds, fruits and seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis evidence suggests β-CAS may also produce γ-glutamyl-β-cyanoalanine as a possible side product/storage form; β-CAS also exhibits low O-acetylserine sulfhydrylase-like cysteine synthase side activity in some species.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; sorghum; maize and other plants
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Sorghum bicolor; Zea mays
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: β-cyanoalanine synthase pathway / cyanide assimilation pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts β-cyanoalanine to asparagine via nitrilase activity and then to aspartate and ammonium via nitrile hydratase-related activity, as summarized in the review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms downstream nitrogen metabolites from β-cyanoalanine
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key second step of the pathway; essential for complete cyanide assimilation, but not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables conversion of β-cyanoalanine into assimilable nitrogen products; mutants show impaired cyanide metabolism, root defects, and drought sensitivity.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; nucleus also detected in some localization studies
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Nitrilase family protein with reported bifunctional nitrilase/nitrile hydratase-like behavior
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NIT4 catalyzes the downstream conversion of β-cyanoalanine in the β-CAS pathway. 2. Arabidopsis mutants lacking NIT4 exhibit impaired cyanide metabolism and developmental/stress phenotypes. 3. The enzyme links cyanide detoxification to asparagine/aspartate/ammonium production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Enzyme assays; mutant analysis; GFP localization; heterologous complementation summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in multiple tissues where β-CAS pathway activity occurs
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Water deficit; endogenous cyanide; pathogen and development-associated cyanide metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review mentions both 'bifunctional nitrilase/nitrile hydratase' and 'bifunctional nitrilase/nitrile reductase', indicating literature-level annotation inconsistency.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis; spinach; potato; maize; vetch; cocklebur; cassava and others
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Spinacia oleracea; Solanum tuberosum; Zea mays; Vicia spp.; Xanthium pennsylvanicum; Manihot esculenta
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: O-acetylserine sulfhydrylase / O-acetylserine thiol-lyase / cysteine synthase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cysteine synthesis in cytosol/chloroplast/mitochondria depending on species
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes cysteine formation from O-acetylserine and sulfide; also shows low β-CAS side activity in some reports.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: parallel sulfur metabolic enzyme related to β-CAS and contributes to cysteine pool
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides cysteine biosynthesis activity and explains some reported cytosolic β-CAS-like activity due to side reactivity.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol; chloroplast; mitochondria depending on isoform/species
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Pyridoxal phosphate-dependent β-substituted alanine synthase family
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OASS is the related cysteine biosynthetic enzyme family member most similar to β-CAS. 2. It can catalyze β-CAS-like activity at low efficiency, explaining some non-mitochondrial cyanide-assimilation signals. 3. The comparison with β-CAS highlights metabolic coupling between cyanide assimilation and sulfur metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical comparisons and localization studies summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Broadly distributed depending on isoform and compartment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core summarized pathway-related gene family in this review because its catalytic overlap is central to interpreting β-CAS localization and sulfur-metabolism linkage.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Released from cytosolic complex with nitrilase-like proteins and translocated to nucleus upon signaling
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates defense gene expression such as Pto kinase after release from nitrilase-like protein complex.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Pto kinase and other defense genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with nitrilase-like proteins in cytosolic complex under normal conditions
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. In a tobacco model summarized in the review, EREBPs associate with nitrilase-like proteins in the cytosol. 2. Upon signaling, EREBPs are released and move to the nucleus. 3. This suggests a regulatory link between β-CAS-pathway-associated nitrilase proteins and defense gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction/localization model summarized from prior literature
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as a mechanistic regulatory interactor of nitrilase-like proteins rather than a direct controller of nutrient metabolite accumulation.
